critic_raw:
1. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ON SECOND-RESOLUTION SENSOR DATA IS CATASTROPHICALLY WRONG. The dataset has sensor readings 'collected every second' over 24 months from over 100 tools. A second-resolution reading is not an independent observation — adjacent seconds for the same tool are nearly identical. A random split will place consecutive seconds from the same machine run into both train and test, producing near-perfect test performance via neighbor leakage. For a 24-month, 1-second-frequency dataset, a single tool generates approximately 63 million observations; the model effectively memorizes each tool's nominal behavior and is tested on frames drawn from the same distribution. This is not a methodological subtlety — it makes the experiment result completely uninterpretable.

2. FAILURE EVENTS ARE RARE AND TEMPORALLY CLUSTERED AROUND ACTUAL FAILURES. In semiconductor manufacturing, equipment failures are clustered: readings in the 60 seconds before failure look systematically different from readings 1 hour before failure. A random split that stratifies on failure/no-failure label will scatter pre-failure readings across train and test. The LSTM trained on pre-failure patterns in training will have seen near-identical pre-failure signatures from the same failure event in training — the actual failure prediction task (detecting early degradation) is not being tested.

3. F1-SCORE IS OPTIMIZED ON AN ARTIFICIAL CLASS BALANCE. Stratification creates a balanced (or at least stratified) train/test distribution. Operational semiconductor manufacturing has failure rates of approximately 0.1-2% of machine-hours. F1-score at the stratified test set balance is not the same as operational F1 at the true prior. The reported improvement in F1 will not translate to the production environment without re-thresholding. Precision at high recall — or AUROC evaluated at the true operating point — would be more informative.

4. LOGISTIC REGRESSION BASELINE WITH 'ENGINEERED TIME-SERIES FEATURES' IS UNDERSPECIFIED. What features? If the logistic regression receives hand-crafted rolling statistics (mean, variance, rate-of-change over a 60-second window), it is a strong baseline that may match LSTM performance on short-horizon prediction. If it receives only static summary statistics, it is trivially weak. The experiment's verdict depends entirely on how competitive the baseline feature engineering is, but this is not specified. Both arms receiving equivalent temporal feature engineering would test architecture; current design tests architecture plus feature richness simultaneously.
all_issues_raised: Second-resolution sensor data random split causes near-perfect leakage from consecutive temporally correlated observations
Pre-failure readings from the same failure event are scattered across train and test, making the actual degradation detection task untested
F1-score evaluated at stratified class balance does not reflect operational performance at true rare failure prior
Logistic regression baseline feature engineering is underspecified, making it impossible to assess whether architecture or feature engineering explains any difference
Stratified random split at window level on second-by-second autocorrelated sensor data causes temporal proximity leakage within the same tool run or failure event
F1-score threshold unspecified at extreme class imbalance; F1 is not comparable across models without fixing the operating threshold
Logistic regression baseline is not the operational comparator for semiconductor fabs; CUSUM, EWMA, and MSPC are the relevant benchmarks
Shift-level stratification within a random split does not prevent operator-behavior leakage; shift-level hold-out required for generalization test
Tool-to-tool sensor variability not addressed: model may learn tool identity rather than failure modes, making it non-transferable to new equipment
Temporal and tool-level leakage from second-level sensor data split at observation level rather than run or tool level
F1 threshold unspecified; at low failure base rates, default 0.5 threshold makes F1 uninformative
Logistic regression baseline time-series features not specified; comparison not reproducible or verifiable
Stratification by production shifts does not address within-shift temporal degradation drift
Prediction horizon and failure label look-ahead window not defined; these choices dominate class balance and practical utility
